text: hello my name is Luis Herrera I am the section head of thoracic surgery at Orlando health in Orlando Florida and I would like to share with you the case of a patient with a t-one be n-one m-zero stage to a non-small cell lung cancer of the left upper lobe on PET scan she had biopsy proven n-one disease in the ten L and eleven L stations the four R and four L stations were negative She presents for a plan for surgical resection, followed by adjuvant therapy.

The surgeon demonstrates pore placement using the robotic XI system, with three 8mm pores in the seventh intercostal space and two 12mm pores, one anterior and one posterior for the assistant. Instruments used include a bipolar dissector, cat ear forceps, and a tip-up grasper. The posterior view reveals the pulmonary artery and left lower lobe bronchus as the surgeon begins to separate the level 10L lymph nodes, carefully maintaining the periaventusial plane to minimize fragmentation and ensure a clean, bloodless dissection.

The surgeon uses a bipolar dissector to safely navigate around the vessels in the subcranial space from the left side, minimizing energy scatter for precise dissection. The field is kept dry while focusing on small vessels supplying the lymph node. The surgeon works proximally along the left main bronchus, with the left main pulmonary artery visible to the left and the vagus nerve and aorta to the right of the screen. The proximal hilum is targeted for complete resection of the suspected metastatic lymph node.

The surgeon uses a glove to pass off the removed lymph nodes, ensuring complete removal. The posterior hilum is explored, with CO₂ insufflation aiding in developing the fissure plane. The surgeon harvests level XI L lymph nodes and exposes the pulmonary artery branches. The lingular artery branch is visible on the left side of the screen, and the posterior segment branch is also identified. The surgical field is cleaned of lymph nodes to facilitate a complete lymphadenectomy and provide space for stapler passage.

The surgeon uses the XI system's control stapler, choosing between anterior and posterior angles for stapling. Dissection continues around the hilar lymph nodes, revealing the artery leading to the apical segment. The lingular artery is carefully dissected, staying close to the vessel wall to avoid lymph node injury and maintain a dry surgical field.

The surgeon uses a caviar forcep through the anterior 12mm trocar to achieve the optimal angle for stapling the vessel. The lingular branch is dissected, and the surgeon creates a window between the superior and inferior pulmonary veins to complete the anterior aspect of the major fissure using a 45mm stapler. A vessel loop is used to retract the parenchyma during this process. The surgeon then performs a meticulous dissection of level XI hilar lymph nodes, placing them in a glove for removal through the assistant trocar due to suspected N-I disease.

The surgeon exposes the oropulmonary window with assistance in retracting the hilum, revealing the plane between the superior pulmonary vein and the left main pulmonary artery. Anterior level X lymph nodes are excised, freeing the superior wall of the pulmonary vein. The vein is divided using a stapler with a posterior approach to avoid collisions with the pulmonary artery. The bifurcation of the first branch of the artery is visible, and the surgeon uses an elastic vessel loop to mobilize a lymph node, flipping the lung forward for better access. The bronchus is then taken.

The surgeon uses a green load stapler from a posterior approach, parallel to the left lower lobe bronchus, to elevate and take the left upper lobe at its takeoff. The hilar nodes have been skeletonized and cleaned out. The surgeon then addresses the calcified AP window nodes at level five, ensuring complete removal due to nodal metastatic disease in the hilum.
The patient went home in two days. Operative time was two hours. Thank you so much for your attention.
